company.name: Valero Energy
company.description: Valero Energy is a U.S.-based independent refiner and renewable fuels producer that manufactures and markets transportation fuels, petrochemical products, ethanol, and renewable diesel.

overview.title: Business mix
paragraphs: Valero operates through refining, renewable diesel, and ethanol segments. Its core economics remain tied to refinery throughput, crude and product spreads, logistics, and compliance-sensitive fuel markets.

The company also participates in renewable fuels through ethanol plants and Diamond Green Diesel, a joint venture with Darling Ingredients that converts recycled fats, used cooking oil, and inedible corn oil into renewable diesel and sustainable aviation fuel.

overview.title: Registry framing
paragraphs: Valero is not a software platform or a consumer electronics company, so its replacement pressure is mostly physical and infrastructural rather than app-level substitution.

The most relevant decentralization pressure comes from electrified transport, distributed energy coordination, local renewable fuel production, feedstock recovery, and cooperative energy infrastructure that can reduce dependence on centralized liquid-fuel supply chains over time.

moatNarrative: Valero's moat is built on scale, refinery complexity, logistics, branded distribution, commodity-trading capability, and regulatory execution. Those assets are expensive, operationally specialized, and hard to replicate at the same reliability standard.

The moat is still cyclical rather than software-like. Refining margins can compress quickly, renewable diesel economics depend heavily on policy and feedstock spreads, and demand destruction from electrification can pressure long-duration fuel volumes.
decentralizationNarrative: Valero's fossil-fuel refining business is structurally centralized: crude sourcing, refining units, pipeline and terminal logistics, and compliance systems all reward large industrial operators.

Renewable diesel and ethanol create a more decentralizable edge because their feedstocks can be locally sourced, but industrial hydrotreating, hydrogen supply, fuel quality standards, and distribution infrastructure still keep the commercial system concentrated. The strongest open-system challenge is therefore demand substitution through distributed electricity and smaller local biofuel loops, not a one-for-one open refinery clone.
company.snapshotNote: Queued from the May 25, 2026 S&P 500 market-cap snapshot ranks 151-175; market and valuation metrics refreshed with late-May 2026 market-data sources.

moat.rationale: Large-scale refining, renewable fuel assets, logistics, compliance expertise, and branded channels create high capital and operational barriers, though commodity cycles limit durability versus software monopolies.

decentralizability.rationale: Most of Valero's present revenue depends on centralized industrial refining and logistics, while renewable fuels and distributed energy can decentralize parts of demand and feedstock supply only gradually.

profitability.rationale: Valero reported $2.348 billion of 2025 net income attributable to stockholders and positive refining and ethanol operating income, but renewable diesel posted a full-year operating loss and overall earnings remain margin-cycle sensitive.

peRatio.rationale: StockAnalysis reported a trailing P/E ratio of 17.96 for Valero as of its late-May 2026 statistics snapshot; refinery earnings are cyclical, so the metric should be treated as a point-in-cycle valuation rather than a stable multiple.

marketCap.rationale: StockAnalysis reported Valero's market capitalization at approximately $73.33 billion as of May 22, 2026, consistent with the queued S&P 500 top-175 snapshot context.

products.name: Valero fuel
products.summary: Valero produces and markets gasoline, diesel, jet fuel, and other refined petroleum products through a large refining and logistics network.
products.whyItMatters: Transportation fuel is the core demand pool that supports Valero's refining economics, and it is the area most exposed to long-run substitution from electrification, demand response, and local energy coordination.
replacementSketch: The practical replacement path is not a small open refinery. It is a gradual shift of transport energy demand from liquid fuels toward electricity coordinated by open standards, local energy markets, and community-scale generation.

Heavy transport, aviation, and legacy vehicle fleets will keep needing liquid fuels for years, so open alternatives are more likely to erode marginal demand and bargaining power than to make Valero's network disappear quickly.

alternatives.name: OpenADR
alternatives.summary: OpenADR is an open demand-response and distributed-energy-resource communication standard for coordinating grid needs with customer-side energy assets.

openness.rationale: OpenADR is explicitly positioned as an open, two-way information exchange model and smart-grid standard.

decentralizationFit.rationale: It supports coordination among utilities, aggregators, customers, demand response, and distributed energy resources rather than relying only on centralized generation and fuel logistics.

readiness.rationale: OpenADR is a live alliance-backed standard with real grid and DER use cases, though deployment still depends on utility programs and compatible devices.

costLeverage.rationale: Demand response and DER coordination can reduce peak costs and fuel demand, but it does not directly replace all liquid-fuel use cases.

alternatives.name: Grid Singularity Exchange
alternatives.summary: Grid Singularity provides tools for simulating and implementing peer-to-peer and community energy trading across local energy markets and microgrids.

openness.rationale: The project describes interoperability and open-source development as part of its technical approach.

decentralizationFit.rationale: Its explicit target is peer-to-peer and community energy trading among prosumers, consumers, energy communities, and microgrids.

readiness.rationale: The software and technical model are credible, but local energy markets remain early and implementation depends on regulation, metering, and grid-operator participation.

costLeverage.rationale: Local market coordination can improve use of local renewables and reduce exposure to centralized energy supply, but it is an indirect substitute for liquid fuels.

disruptionConcepts.name: Local energy markets for transport electrification
disruptionConcepts.summary: Community energy markets, open demand-response protocols, and DER coordination can make local electricity a more useful transport fuel, especially for EV charging, fleet depots, and shared charging infrastructure.
disruptionConcepts.thesis: If more transport energy is bought from local solar, storage, flexible loads, and community microgrids, centralized liquid-fuel suppliers lose marginal demand and some local pricing power.
disruptionConcepts.bitcoinOrDecentralizationRole: Decentralization matters through local market operation, open standards, and community energy coordination rather than through Bitcoin. The critical shift is from refinery-to-station logistics toward many small energy producers and flexible loads coordinating at the distribution edge.
disruptionConcepts.coordinationMechanism: Households, fleet operators, building owners, EV chargers, batteries, and aggregators publish available generation, demand flexibility, and charging needs into local market or demand-response systems.
disruptionConcepts.verificationOrTrustModel: Smart meters, charger telemetry, settlement records, and grid-operator constraints verify delivered energy and prevent participants from claiming nonexistent generation or flexibility. The weak point is that most implementations still require trusted metering and regulated settlement layers.
failureModes: Distribution-grid constraints and utility rules may block peer-to-peer settlement even when software is ready.
Liquid fuels remain superior for many heavy-duty, aviation, emergency, and long-range use cases.
Customer-side hardware, metering, and interconnection costs can slow adoption.
adoptionPath: Start with fleet depots, workplaces, and multifamily buildings where charging demand and local solar/storage can be coordinated behind or near one meter.
Expand into community energy markets and utility demand-response programs that reward flexible charging and local renewable use.
Use interoperable protocols to let chargers, batteries, buildings, and aggregators participate without single-vendor lock-in.

decentralizationFit.rationale: The concept directly shifts coordination from centralized fuel supply to distributed generation, storage, flexible demand, and local market participants.

coordinationCredibility.rationale: OpenADR and local-energy-market research show credible coordination primitives, but settlement and distribution-grid governance remain hard.

implementationFeasibility.rationale: The pieces exist for pilots and fleet-scale deployments, while broad retail substitution still depends on EV penetration, metering, regulation, and grid upgrades.

incumbentPressure.rationale: The pressure is meaningful for gasoline and diesel demand growth, but slow turnover of vehicles and hard-to-electrify transport limit near-term impact.

products.name: Diamond Green Diesel
products.category: Renewable diesel and sustainable aviation fuel
products.summary: Diamond Green Diesel is Valero's 50/50 joint venture with Darling Ingredients that processes recycled animal fats, used cooking oil, and inedible corn oil into renewable diesel and sustainable aviation fuel.
products.whyItMatters: It is Valero's main renewable diesel platform and one of the clearest places where waste-feedstock collection, policy incentives, hydrogen supply, and industrial scale determine whether low-carbon liquid fuels remain centralized or become more locally contestable.
replacementSketch: A decentralized replacement would not mirror DGD's billion-gallon industrial hydrotreating footprint. It would combine local waste-oil collection, smaller biodiesel or biogas systems, open process documentation, and cooperative fleet demand.
This is a partial substitute at best: renewable diesel compatible with existing engines has quality and infrastructure advantages that small biodiesel systems may not match.

alternatives.name: Open Source Ecology Biofuel
alternatives.summary: Open Source Ecology documents small-scale biofuel concepts and related open hardware pathways, including biodiesel, biogas, gasifiers, and other fuel-production approaches.

openness.rationale: The OSE wiki is openly published and explicitly organized around open-source technology documentation.

decentralizationFit.rationale: Small-scale biofuel documentation and related open hardware fit local production and repair loops better than centralized refinery models.

readiness.rationale: The documentation is useful for experimentation and education, but it is not a polished, compliance-ready substitute for industrial renewable diesel supply.

costLeverage.rationale: Local waste-oil or biomass fuel loops can reduce some fuel purchases where feedstock is available, but quality control, labor, methanol inputs, and compliance limit scale.

disruptionConcepts.name: Cooperative waste-oil-to-fuel loops
disruptionConcepts.summary: Local restaurants, farms, workshops, municipal fleets, and cooperatives could coordinate waste-oil collection and small-scale fuel production for limited diesel-equivalent demand, using open documentation and shared quality controls.
disruptionConcepts.thesis: The concept attacks the feedstock and local-demand edge of renewable diesel rather than Valero's full refinery stack, turning some waste streams into community-controlled fuel or energy inputs.
disruptionConcepts.bitcoinOrDecentralizationRole: Decentralization matters through cooperative ownership, local feedstock recovery, shared production equipment, and open process knowledge. Bitcoin is not central unless used later for settlement among participants.
disruptionConcepts.coordinationMechanism: Feedstock suppliers register waste-oil availability, collection operators verify pickup, processors publish batch outputs and quality tests, and fleet users commit offtake through cooperative contracts.
disruptionConcepts.verificationOrTrustModel: Pickup receipts, container weights, batch logs, basic fuel-quality tests, and fleet performance records constrain false reporting. Stronger systems could add tamper-resistant scale tickets and third-party lab sampling for cooperative members.
failureModes: Small biodiesel systems may fail cold-flow, storage, warranty, or emissions requirements for many fleets.
Feedstock supply is local and finite, so the model cannot replace broad highway, aviation, or industrial fuel demand.
Poor process control can damage engines or create safety and environmental risks.
adoptionPath: Begin with non-road equipment, farm use, or controlled municipal pilots where fuel quality and warranty exposure can be managed.
Standardize open batch records, collection logs, and cooperative offtake agreements.
Use successful local loops to reduce waste-disposal costs and substitute a small share of diesel purchases where regulations allow.

decentralizationFit.rationale: The model is explicitly local, cooperative, and feedstock-recovery based, though it only addresses a narrow part of the liquid-fuel market.

coordinationCredibility.rationale: Waste-oil collection and cooperative offtake are straightforward, but durable verification and fuel-quality governance are nontrivial.

implementationFeasibility.rationale: Small-scale biodiesel is technically plausible, but compliance, safety, fuel quality, and engine compatibility make publishable deployment harder than documentation alone suggests.

incumbentPressure.rationale: The concept can pressure niche local volumes and waste-feedstock economics, but it is unlikely to threaten Valero's industrial renewable diesel scale near term.

summaryMarkdown: Prepared a new Valero Energy registry bundle with two products: Valero fuel and Diamond Green Diesel. The refresh frames Valero as a high-moat, capital-intensive refiner whose strongest decentralization pressure comes from distributed energy coordination, local energy markets, and narrow cooperative waste-fuel loops rather than direct open-source refinery substitution.
